sentence.text: CR
"Lola, tell me why you are in such a bad mood."
CR
CR
"Well, I keep trying to do this math and I just can't do it."
CR
CR
"Well, it’s no good asking me, is it?
I’m no good at doing things like that.
But I don't need to tell you that, do I Lola?"
CR
"No, I don’t think you do.
When you add 2 and 2 you seem to get five."
CR
"Hmm, yes.
But that keeps me well away from anything to do with math, and that's the way I like it."
CR
"Maybe you're the smart kid, after all.
But just let me get on with what I have to do for the class test."
CR
CR
"What a good spot this is for a camp site."
CR
CR
"Yes, we love it for the fishing.
This park can get very busy but we avoid those peak times.
Do you want to help put up the tent?
We have to be sure to lock the food away from the bears and even Bigfoot."
CR
CR
"Bigfoot?
That's just a tall story.
You don't get ten foot tall apes in America."
CR
CR
"That’s what you think.
You come over here and take a look at these marks on the ground.
What do you think of the tall story now?"
CR
My sister has a cat called Ritz.
With a name like that it sounds as if it must be a very fine cat with good manners.
No way!
That cat is a mean, cold hunter.
CR
CR
Last week, I saw it crawling across the lawn.
When I had a second look, I saw it had a mouse in its jaws.
It was awful.
It looked at me and then it ran off.
CR
CR
Next time I saw it, it was sitting on the grass in the sunshine.
It was blinking and yawning and washing its paws.
It gave me a look as if to say, "What a lovely day it is for a cat to sit in the sun and be good!"
It really looked like Ritz was smiling at me.
CR
CR
The sun is up and Jim is up.
Jim’s dad has a red truck but it has a lot of mud on it.
Jim thinks he will wax the truck.
CR
CR
He gets the mud off with a wet rag.
Then he gets the lid off the can of wax.
He puts a lot of wax on the truck.
CR
CR
Then Jim sits in the sun as he is hot!
He thinks the wax can come off and gets his rag.
The truck is big and red and dad gives Jim a hug.
CR
Tom has a dog, Zip.
Tom sends his dog off on a run and tells him not to get wet.
CR
CR
Oh no! The hut is a mess and Tom is mad at Zip.
We will have to give Zip a bath.
And, we have to try and get rid of this mess in the hut.
CR
CR
Tom will have to try and settle that dog.
But I think Zip is full of fun.
I think I will get a dog like Zip, as well.
CR
CR
Dan has gone off to see his pal.
Will you come with me to see them as well?
You will soon get there if you run.
I can give you a hand if the hill is too steep.
CR
Come on, I can pull you up.
There, that’s not too bad is it?
Let’s run to get there soon.
Dan will have gone soon and I have to see him.
Mom will get mad if I miss him.
CR
CR
See, there's Dan in the green hut.
He keeps his things in that hut at the end of the path.
CR
I think I will ask that boy if he will lend us his ball and bat.
I can give him this bottle of soda if he will let us have the ball.
He can watch us to see we don't keep his ball.
Come on then, send the ball to me.
I will bat.
CR
CR
All set!
Toss the ball to me and give it a spin.
That was a dud!
All set?
Run and get that!
I tag you- Out!
If I am out, I think I have had it.
Let's give the boy his ball back and sit on that log.
CR
This was a very funny dream.
There was a big fat cat in it, but he was green.
He did not like milk.
I had a can of cat meat for him there too, but he didn't like that.
CR
CR
He wanted to eat green grass and wood all the time.
He seemed to like thick, dry, wood.
Then he let some wood drop on my hand and I woke up.
CR
CR
There was my dog with her bone.
I had gone to sleep at the foot of the tree in the hot sun.
That was a funny cat, and a funny dream.
CR
CR
Did you say you can fix my bike for me?
I will be pleased if you can.
You see, I can't get home on foot.
I live too far away and need my bike to get there.
Mom is at work till five so she can't get me.
I'm in a fix.
CR
CR
I think a little bit of glass made a hole in the tube.
If you can mend it, I will soon be on my way.
You have been very good to take the time to do this for me.
Thanks and have a good day.
CR
CR
It's hot and dry today.
The summer has come at last.
Maybe my Mom will say it's OK to take my bike and some food to the river.
I will ask Sara if she wants to come, too.
CR
There is not a cloud in the sky.
The sun looks as if it is here for ever.
Come on, Mom!
Let's put the food in the backpack.
CR
CR
Give me a soda to drink and away I go!
All I have to do is get Sara but I bet she is still in bed, fast asleep.
CR
Madison and Noah have set off to go fishing.
The sun is shining and the air is warm.
They choose a good place beside the brook where the bank is not very steep.
This time they are hoping to get a good catch.
CR
After a long time they still hadn't gotten any fish, so they begin to shoot the breeze.
CR
CR
"I think this is the wrong place," says Madison.
CR
CR
"Just down there where that tree trunk sticks out over the water looks much better.
That's the sort of dark place where the fish will breed."
Then she gets up to have a look.
CR
CR
"Maybe," says Noah, "but remember last time when I tried to stretch out too far and fell in?
I got very wet, and my rod was broken.
You can stick your neck out and be brave if you want to.
But don't blame me if you fall in.
Have a look at those black clouds.
There's a breeze coming up and it will start to rain soon."
CR
I was just a greenhorn American soldier that fateful year.
We had fought the British army and lost and we were worn out.
The General found us a valley for our winter ground but it brought us no peace.
CR
That was a bitter, bitter winter.
We were holed up in rough cabins made of logs, with cracks so big the wind just howled straight through the walls.
The winds were sharp and cold and we only had rags to wear.
The only pillows were our knapsacks and our only blankets the snow clouds above us.
Few of us had any shoes.
We made do with raw hide boots which scratched and cut our feet and made them bleed.
We left red trails in the show.
CR
Some days there was nothing to eat but stale bread, and even that we had to share.
For days on end we lived on salt pork alone.
The folks in Congress sent no money as they had none to spare.
I have never been so hungry in all my life.
We all grew sick with hunger and cold.
We huddled in our cabins, too weak to stand.
Some brave soldiers couldn't make it through the hardship.
CR
It seemed like our fight for freedom had to die with us.
But in the spring, we got a break from the French and we had some training in fighting.
We got guns and we got stronger in body and in spirit.
We were not going to yield to anyone, ever again.
China has far more people than any other country.
It claims, with good cause, to have the longest history in the world.
CR
CR
In the England of King Alfred, men could write and copy books.
They did this by hand with pen and with paint brush.
In China, at the same time, they could print books by using wood-cut blocks.
Long before the birth of Christ, the Chinese had trade-routes to Tibet and India.
CR
Then for hundreds of years China and its rulers had little contact with the rest of the world.
Right across the north of their land they still had the Great Wall of China.
This had been built almost two thousand years ago.
It crosses hills, valleys, rivers, rocks and sand.
Its total length is about five thousand miles.
This gigantic structure is known as the one of the Seven Wonders of the World.
But it is not true that it can be seen from the moon.
CR
CR
But things have now changed.
China has become a world power.
It has many new factories, roads, bridges and dams.
More and more tourists are visiting China and learning about the history of the Chinese people.
CR
Ryan and Hunter were resting on the hillside on their way home from baseball practice.
Hunter suddenly pointed down the valley.
"Look at that idiot with that bus.
Can you imagine bringing a school bus down a country road like this?
There's no room for it.
What if something comes the other way?"
CR
Just then they saw a car coming much too fast in the other direction.
The school bus and the car went towards each other without either driver knowing it.
The bus and the car almost hit each other at the sharp bend.
The car turned right and smashed partly through a gate and partly through a fence.
The two friends snapped into action.
CR
Ryan tried to phone for help, but there was no signal for his cell phone in such a remote area.
CR
CR
Ryan and Hunter ran faster than they had ever run before.
They were scared of children being hurt and what they might find.
Hunter remained by the crash, while Ryan sprinted to the nearest town.
It was a good thing he was fit.
At the coffee shop he dialled 911 and got through right away.
CR
"There’s been an accident about a mile down the road.
A school bus and a car are smashed up and some people are hurt.
This is an emergency."
CR
CR
The operator said, "Tell me where the crash happened and we'll get help to you real soon."
CR
CR
"I'll go back and tell them," replied Ryan, and he was soon running back up the road.
CR
The dark streets are empty.
Plate glass windows let me see the counter of the all-night diner where one man sits alone, hunched.
There is a couple sitting on the other side of the counter.
They appear to be in the middle of speaking to each other.
The lights are harsh and make the room look sharp and cold.
It is still, very still, like something is about to happen.
CR
I go in and take a seat.
I sit, so I can hear the couple, but watch the man.
He can see me clearly, too.
I tip my hat back and sag slightly on the stool.
CR
CR
"I'll have a coffee," I say to the guy behind the counter.
CR
CR
"Do I know you?" asks the man.
CR
CR
Growing crops for food matters to the US.
In 1870, 70-80 percent of the workers in the US were in farming.
Today, it is just 2-3 percent.
Farmers have become better at getting more food from the same amount of land.
CR
The main job on a farm is to grow and raise food for the US.
They grow many things from corn and salad crops to peanuts.
They also have many dairy and beef cattle, and pigs.
CR
CR
Children used to work on farms where they lived.
Not many of them do that now.
There are laws in place to make sure that children who live on farms come to no harm.
CR
CR
A large farm that raises cattle can be known as a ranch.
A ranch can be hundreds of miles wide.
It lets the cattle roam and feed across the land.
Some ranchers used to be known as cowboys.
They take care of the cattle and make sure they are safe.
Then, they have to round up the cattle and go to market.
CR
Now, many years ago, Paul Bunyan was born in Maine.
He was so big his howls made fish jump and his clapping made windows shake.
He was so big he had to curl up in a lumber truck to sleep.
His parents took him to live in the woods where he grew up.
CR
One winter it was so cold the snow was blue.
Paul was staring at this odd sight.
He found an animal stuck in a snow drift.
It was a baby ox, so he brought it back home with him.
The next day, the ox was warm and dry but it had blue hair.
Paul kept his blue ox and called him Babe.
Soon, Babe was the biggest ox there was.
Paul had Babe help with his work and they made a very good team.
CR
CR
Paul and Babe went on to live and work in the logging camps.
Paul set up his own camp near the banks of a river.
The camp was so big, you needed a basket of food with you to walk from one side to the other.
CR
CR
People say Paul Bunyan made the shape of this land.
Others say he and Babe cut down all the trees from North and South Dakota.
Some say, he went as far as Alaska.
It all rests on the teller and the tale.
CR
I like coming to visit Abigail.
I am so glad to get away from the city with all its crowds and noise.
All those cars, buses and trucks make noise and smells.
They can knock you down in the blink of an eye.
You have to get out of the way as quick as you can.
Out here it is full of trees and animals.
But you have to watch out for the skunks, because they stink.
CR
It is the most fun when we come to visit in July.
We come every year for the picnic and fireworks.
Before the fireworks we have hot dogs, coleslaw, potato salad and root beer.
First, I have to help my aunt frost the flag cake.
Then we all wear red, white and blue.
We watch the parade and then get ready for the fireworks.
CR
I get to sit high up on the edge of the fire truck to watch.
Those hoses are lumpy but I don't mind.
Up here I can see it all.
I have a great view to the shore.
I can also lay back and see all the jewels in the sky.
You can hike all day in the Blue Ridge Mountains.
The air when you are walking on the trail is fresh and clear.
It smells like green trees and wild flowers.
The path is dark green and little light reaches there.
It is so thick with trees you cannot see the sky.
From far away, the early morning air looks like smoke.
It makes the color of the mountains a deep blue, like fog.
When the sun rises higher, some of the water in the air will burn off.
Then the mountains will slowly turn dark green.
CR
The mountain water is icy cold and sparkles in the sun.
You could drink it all day - it's that good!
The rough landscape takes your breath away with its scale and color.
Hikers should stick to the trails for a safe passage through the miles of wild, tough land.
You have to go there to smell and taste the huge backdrop that the mountains give to you.
The size of them is hard to take in from maps and books.
CR
CR
All too soon it will be time to leave.
It will be hard to go back to the world of cars, roads, stores and crowds of people.
But you will remember your time in the Blue Ridge Mountains.
CR
CR
Over the years there have been many songs made up about them, often performed by well-known artists.
CR
Every year many people go hiking.
Their dream is to live a simple life off the beaten path for a short time.
For some backpackers, the complete lack of cars and trucks on the trails suits them best.
CR
CR
Each part of the country has many different trails.
Some trails are short and take only a day to walk.
These trails might be near your home.
You could take bread, cold cuts and drinks for a picnic.
CR
It's great to get the feeling of open spaces and blue skies.
Not having to carry a heavy pack which weighs a ton is good too.
Or you could pick one of the other trails which are tougher.
You can walk at a great height up into the mountains.
Or you could wind your way down to sea level over a week or so.
The oldest of these longer backpacking trails begins in Maine and ends in Georgia.
CR
CR
The choice is yours, if you go for a short walk or a long hike.
Take a deep breath and head off.
The fun thing is to have nothing in front of you but the open trail.
When you have had enough you can go back to the city.
CR
CR
Cole had given his neighbor a promise that he would keep an eye on her house while she was away.
When he went there that night with Dylan, it was dark and pretty cold, with a nip of frost in the air.
Inside the house was cold too, but everything was in order.
Suddenly, the boys heard a noise outside.
CR
CR
"What's that?", said Dylan.
CR
CR
"I don’t know," said Cole, grabbing a stick.
"But you go out that way and I’ll go this way and we can find out."
CR
CR
After waiting a little while, Cole went out into the darkness.
He went very slowly and carefully around the end of the house.
Then he heard Dylan shout out and say, "Hey, Cole, come and look at this."
CR
Cole took a few quick steps towards Dylan.
All at once something bumped into Cole’s legs.
There was a shrill cry and Cole could not help stumbling.
As he fell forward, he put his arms up.
Cole’s hands and his stick hit Dylan smack in the face, and they both fell down.
They yelled and got to their feet as quickly as they could.
CR
CR
"You fool!" shouted Dylan.
"I only wanted to show you where the cat had put her kittens.
Now, I’ll have a black eye.
Ow, and I'm going to have a bruise on my face!"
CR
The Native Tribes of the US have lived here since before the end of the last ice age.
Groups settled many parts of the land and each group had their own way of life.
They became the Tribes of the Great Plains.
The plains stretch straight through the center of the country.
They cross rivers and deserts.
In some parts, a person can see for hundreds of miles over flat lands.
Large stretches of this land used to be covered by high, thick grasses.
CR
CR
The grass fed the buffulo that were the center of the lives of the Great Plains Tribes.
The animals gave them meat, and much more.
Buffalo were central to their spirit life as well.
The horse played an important part in the life of the Tribes too.
The horse gave them a new way to travel.
It allowed them to follow and hunt the buffalo more safely.
CR
The coming of white settlers to the Great Plains changed things.
It saw the end of the horse and buffalo life of the Tribes.
Settlers did not want buffalo to destroy their crops and killed many of them.
The Native Tribes struggled with this threat to their way of life and fought it.
This way of life came to an end when they were forced to live on limited lands.
CR
CR
Do you know what a blackbird is?
I bet you're thinking of a bird that looks like a crow.
Perhaps you know the song about four and twenty blackbirds baked in a pie.
CR
CR
But there's another kind of blackbird.
It's a spy plane and is one of the most powerful aircraft seen by mankind.
It can travel up to three times the speed of sound.
It is shaped smoothly to reduce its radar footprint.
It is able to fly from the west coast to the east in one hour and four minutes.
This makes it the fastest aircraft in the world.
The Blackbird was used by the government for twenty-five years.
It has been used by NASA for experiments.
Some experts say that the Blackbird was more useful than anything in space because no one knew it was there.
It would sneak up behind you and be gone before you knew it.
CR
CR
Just 32 of the aircraft were built between 1966 and 1998.
You can see a Blackbird now in the Air and Space Center.
CR
We have had many pioneers in our country and some have had a big impact on us all, like Henry Ford.
CR
CR
When he was young, Ford was curious and tinkered with things.
As an adult, Ford became an engineer in Detroit.
He was able to combine his time and money to work with car engines.
CR
CR
In 1903, Henry Ford started the Ford Motor Company.
He reached his dream of making a car that was affordable and reliable when he made the Model T in 1908.
This car was very popular.
CR
CR
Ford changed car-making completely in 1913.
He made a moving assembly line.
This took the car parts by moving belt to the workers.
The speed was constant to keep the line running smoothly.
His clever idea in car-making reduced substantially the time it took to make a car, and made it cheap to make.
CR
CR
Ford's company became the largest car-maker in the world because there was no competition that could beat the Model T.
Ford's company helped to change America because cities changed and suburbs grew; the highway system expanded.
We connected to a love of the open road.
Ford saw many of these changes before his death in 1947.
CR
CR
In deep sea diving the human body goes a long way under water.
The body feels pressure which is related to the depth it is under the water.
The weak parts of the human body which can be particularly affected by this are the ears and the lungs.
CR
If a diver descends to a very deep level of water, there is real danger.
Because of pressure, our bodies are unable to get rid of the nitrogen which we breathe in.
At this depth the nitrogen being absorbed into the bloodstream begins to affect the brain.
The diver becomes confused and gets into a condition described as "raptures of the deep".
CR
If he then comes quickly to the surface, the nitrogen will cause his blood to bubble, and he can die.
This danger is known as the "bends".
The only way to avoid the bends used to be for the diver to come up to the surface very slowly.
The modern method is for him to go into a special tank called a decompression chamber.
CR
In this chamber the pressure is the same as it was under water.
Very gradually the pressure is adjusted, until it is equal to that at ground level.
CR
CR
So the next time you dive, and feel the pressure on your ears, watch out!
CR
Martin Luther King Jr. came from a humble family, and like the inventor Edison or the businessman Ford, his name was to become a household word.
He was the most important voice in the movement of equal rights for all.
He was famous for using nonviolent methods to stand against injustice.
He did all he could to make us realize that "all men are created equal".
The history of his work gives an indication of the enormity of his achievements.
CR
CR
King believed that as a Christian pastor he had to be nonviolent.
He believed that violent protests would ruin the struggle for change.
King and others were willing to go to jail for the cause of civil rights.
Others criticized him for his conviction.
In 1963, King led mass protests against the practice of discrimination in Birmingham, Alabama.
King was arrested and jailed for his part in the civil protests.
After he was released from jail, King took part in the huge civil rights march on Washington DC in August 1963.
This is where he delivered his famous "I have a dream" speech.
He predicted a time when freedom and equality for all would become a reality in America.
CR
He was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1964.
After that, he led the movement to increase voter sign-up among the African-American population.
Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated on April 4, 1968 during a visit to Memphis, Tennessee.
His birthday is observed as a national holiday on the third Monday in January.
He is one of the foremost names in American history.
CR
On July 2, 1900, a man introduced a new engineering wonder to the world.
The machines to which he gave his name were expensive to build.
As it turned out, they were also delicate.
CR
CR
His name was Count Zeppelin.
No one in the USA had heard of him at that time.
However, the Zeppelin was to become a household word.
CR
The Zeppelin was a giant rigid airship, which was filled with hydrogen gas.
Zeppelins were driven by two engines which were fixed below it.
But the engines of the first Zeppelin were far too small to be effective.
It had to be taken apart.
Zeppelin 2 wasn't much better and was destroyed in a storm.
But the Zeppelin 3 design worked well.
The last Zeppelin, called the Hindenburg, was number 129.
CR
CR
By the 1920s, Britain, France, Italy and the USA were all building airships, but the Hindenburg impressed the whole world.
The Hindenburg was 800 feet long and 135 feet wide.
This is four times longer than a 747 airplane.
CR
In 1937, the Hindenburg made a flight across the Atlantic to the USA.
Thousands of people came to watch its landing.
The beautiful, gleaming, airship came gently down to its landing point.
Suddenly, it gave a jerk.
There was a flash of flame and it exploded.
A moment later its whole length crumpled to the ground.
Its crew and passengers all died.
CR
CR
Mia, Luis and Diego entered the back workshop of the garage.
They saw Tomas having an argument with a boy who was sitting on a motorbike.
"Look, Pablo" said Tomas, "That’s not your bike, and you can’t ride one anyhow, so get off."
CR
The motorbike was on its rear stand in the middle of the workshop.
Pablo shrugged his shoulders and said, "Look, that’s the front brake, that’s the clutch, that’s the gearbox and here’s the ignition."
Then he turned on the ignition and the engine started up.
CR
CR
"Get off!" shouted Tomas.
CR
CR
"It’s easy!" yelled Pablo, over the engine noise.
He suddenly gave a jerk, and hit the gearshift as the bike bumped forward off its stand.
They all knew in a flash that Pablo meant to ride the bike out of the garage.
CR
The rear wheel of the bike came straight down on to a pool of oil.
The wheel spun around, and the bike slid to one side and then suddenly pulled away.
It went straight at the corner of the garage.
The front wheel hit the leg of a bench and the bike bounced back before it lurched again.
Then it fell over, throwing Pablo to one side.
CR
The engine stalled.
All at once there was silence.
Pablo got to his feet and scowled at the others.
They were laughing so hard they couldn’t speak.
He was red in the face and covered with blotches of oil and dirt.
CR
Doctors worked on Anne's eyes to get some of her sight back.
She worked hard at school and became a star scholar.
One of the most important techniques that she learned there was the hand alphabet for deaf people.
For a person who is deaf-blind, words are shaped on the surface of the hand.
CR
CR
In 1886, the teacher in charge of the school suggested to Anne that she work with a child who had been deaf-blind since she was a baby.
This six year old girl was called Helen Keller.
She could not hear, could not see and had no language.
CR
CR
Anne said yes.
Helen was not an easy student and it was a challenge to get her to learn.
But Anne had a strong character and was determined that she would not give up on Helen.
Soon, Helen learned her first word by using the hand alphabet.
After that, Helen began to learn with speed and efficiency.
CR
CR
Later on, Anne also taught Helen how to speak.
When Helen went to college in 1900, Anne went with her to translate her books into hand language.
They worked as a team for the rest of Anne's life.
CR
CR
Helen Keller became well known.
She said that Anne was the one who made her fame possible.
Helen said that Anne was the one who deserved the praise.
Anne had found a way to teach Helen and gave her the freedom to live a whole life.
CR
Thomas Edison’s first recording machine was a simple sort of thing.
It used a roll of metal to record on.
He spoke a few words of a nursery rhyme into it, and then set it to play back to him.
The funny, squeaky voice on the machine spoke to him quite clearly.
It said, "Mary had a little lamb, its fleece was white as snow."
That was well over a hundred years ago in 1877.
CR
CR
He competed with others to be the first to design and build a machine that could record and play back sound.
Edison got there first.
CR
Edison is, of course, famous for other inventions.
He never stopped making things.
He made an electric light bulb, and switches and fuses.
He also designed a machine which could make electric power.
He spent a lot of time improving the working of the telephone and the telegraph.
He helped to design cameras for film-making.
CR
CR
He played a part in the invention of the typewriter.
He designed new ways to improve the making of cement and concrete.
He build an electric train.
He was still hard at work and full of new ideas when he was 80 years old.
CR
CR
One of the most amazing things about Thomas Edison was that he had no training as a scientist.
He had great problems learning in school and left early, without a diploma.
He was self-taught after that.
He is one of a very few such men in history.
They were men who had a kind of genius for inventing things.
CR
CR
When a human heart comes to a stop the person dies.
That is simply because the heart is a pump.
If it stops, the body, which depends on that pump, also stops.
It is this pump which keeps our blood on the move, from the end of each toe to the top of the head.
CR
CR
As blood circulates it collects carbon dioxide.
This is the waste matter in the blood stream.
The carbon dioxide makes the blood darker as it is collected.
The blood then takes on the more purple color, and it is this that we can see showing in our veins.
CR
CR
The blood returns to the right chamber of the heart, from where it is pumped to the lungs for cleaning.
Then it carries on to the left chamber of the heart.
From there the clean blood is pumped out once more to circulate around the body.
This constant pumping and cleaning goes on without a stop until we die.
CR
CR
More and more people now live to be 100 years old.
That means their hearts have been pumping non-stop for a century.
We can only wonder when a computer will be built that can last that long!
The movies used to be in black and white with no sound.
Then in the 1920's there were considerable changes in how films were made.
CR
CR
Once actors could talk on screen it changed their performances.
Soon sound and color in films were the norm.
As films became more popular, the film makers had a great deal of power.
They controlled the actors, the theaters and ticket sales.
CR
CR
By 1939 there were more movie theaters in the US than there were banks.
Hollywood was the main center of film making in the world, not just in the USA.
The Golden Age of film ended when the Supreme Court ruled this control had to change.
More changes came when TV found a place in all homes.
Theaters were empty and the business went into decline.
CR
CR
Yet, the medium of film did not die.
There were still people who liked to watch a film on a night out.
The movie business used new technology and changed the way films were made and shown.
Wide screens and drive-ins were all part of the show.
They made sure that they could give you something you couldn't get on TV.
CR
CR
Now, film makers use DVDs and gaming to do the same thing.
The movies are a big business and have come a long way from the orange groves of Hollywood.
CR
After the war with Mexico (1846-1848), the homestead movement got bigger.
People who came to the States from Europe were ambitious to make a new life so moved west.
When the South left the Union, a major stumbling block to homesteading was removed.
CR
CR
In 1862, the Homestead Act passed into law.
The new law had three steps to owning land: filing a claim, improving the land, and filing for deed of title.
Any U.S. citizen could apply and in exchange for improving the land and building a dwelling, the homesteader could file for the deed to own the land after 5 years.
Physical existence for the homesteader was very hard.
Crops were blown down by strong winds and blizzards in the winter.
The summers were scorching and had swarms of insects.
Fuel and water supplies were short, so simple cooking and heating chores were hard.
The plots on the dry plains were too small to grow large crops.
The landscape made grazing livestock on the prairie difficult .
In many places, the experiment failed and the homesteader did not stay on the land long enough to make a final claim.
CR
However, homesteaders who toughed it out were rewarded with excellent chances for wealth and a new life.
By 1934, over 1.6 million homestead claims had gone through the system and more than 270 million acres given over.
In 1976 the Homestead Act was stopped in 48 states, but homesteading was allowed in Alaska until 1986.
The state of Nebraska has the national memorial to the Homestead Act.
CR
The oil industry grew as a direct result of the motor car.
More precisely, it was because of the internal combustion engine, which burns the gasoline made from oil.
Oil is formed from the remains of plants and creatures living millions of years ago and the weight of tons of rock pressed on those remains. This caused a gradual change.
CR
CR
The liquid, which has been trapped and compressed under the weight of rock above it, rushes to the surface.
To obtain the oil a gigantic drill works its way down from the surface and breaks into the lake of oil.
The liquid, which has been trapped and compressed under the weight of the rock above it, rushes to the surface.
There are two main problems for the oil industry.
First of all, scientists have to locate the oil-bearing rocks.
Then, the oil engineers have to drill at exactly the right spot.
Some drills have to drill into the ground as deep as four miles down before oil is found.
When the oil is found, it has to be moved, often by pipeline.
Some pipelines are over 1,000 miles in length.
One of the largest pipelines in the world runs through Alaska.
CR
Second, we now know that the sources of crude oil will run dry.
Our way of life depends on oil.
Large amounts of oil are used in industry, for fuel and to run cars.
When the oil runs out we will need to replace it with things such as solar and nuclear power.
But each of these has its own drawbacks.
This means that finding a replacement for crude oil is a slow process.
